coredata.dc:title: Unlocking the potential of borophene nanoribbons for efficient hydrogen storage

coredata.dc:description: This research explores the structural, electronic, optical, and hydrogen storage properties of borophene nanoribbons (BNRs) with armchair (ANR-B-H) and zigzag (ZNR B-H) edges. Computational simulations optimized these structures, revealing that 7ZNR-B-H has a superior binding energy. Chemical modifications, such as fluorine passivation and functionalization, influenced bond parameters and quantum properties. Bilayer BNRs showed increased stability and enhanced electrical conductivity. Our study demonstrated promising hydrogen storage capabilities, with passivated and functionalized BNRs achieving suitable adsorption energies and a significant gravimetric storage capacity of 20.32 wt%, exceeding DOE standards. NH2 functionalization notably improved adsorption energy, enhancing potential for efficient hydrogen storage. Changes in absorption spectra post-H2 adsorption further highlight BNRs’ potential for hydrogen storage applications. These findings provide valuable insights into BNRs, paving the way for their use in electronic devices and hydrogen storage systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, and b) Borophene nanoribbons with an armchair edge (ANR-B-H) and (c, and d) zigzag edge (ZNR-B-H). The chemical modifications by edge passivation with fluoride (7ZNR-B-F) and functionalization (7ZNR-B-H-CO) are shown in (e) and (f) respectively. The front and side view of 7ZNR-B-H is shown in (g).
The spin up/down total density of states (a–d) and the corresponding HOMO/LUMO distributions of 3ANR-B-H. 3ZNR-B-H, 4ANR-B-H, and 7ZNR-B-H.

Partial density of states of 7ZNR-B after passivation and functionalization with F, SH, OH, CHO, and CN (a–e) and the related HOMO/LUMO are given in (f–j). The vertical dotted line is the Fermi level.

(a–k) One H2 molecule is adsorbed by the optimized structure of 7ZNR-B and its passivated/functionalized counterparts at a location near those counterparts. 7ZNR-B-H (l–s) adsorbed 3H2, 5H2, 7H2, 10H2, 13H2, 20H2, 40H2, and 50 H2 molecules.

(a) 3ANR-B-H, 4ANR-B-H, 3ZNR-B-H, 7ZNR-B-H, 7ZNR-B-F, and 7ZNR-B-OH absorption spectra (b), The functionalized 7ZNR-B-H absorption spectrum (c) illustrates how the adsorption of various concentrations of H2 molecules changes the absorption spectra of 7ZNR-B-H.
Some important quantum parameters like bond length (Å), dihedral, and bond angles (degrees) for ANR-B-H, ZNR-B-H, and the derivatives of 7ZNR-B.

The HOMO energy (EH), the LUMO energy (EL), chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), chemical hardness (η), dipole moment (μ), the electrical conductivity (σ), energy gap (Eg) and the binding energy (BE) for ANR-B-H, ZNR-B-H and the derivatives of 7ZNR-B.
The adsorption energy (Ea), adsorption length (d), and the desorption temperature (TD) of hydrogen on 7NR-B-H and its derivatives.
Gravimetric storage capacity (wt%) for 7ZNR-B-H and the other materials with the references.

The calculated excited state (ES), maximum wavelength (λmax), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for 3ANR-B-H, 4ANR-B-H, 3ZNR-B-H, 7ZNR-B-H, 7ZNR-B-H after passivation and functionalization, and 7ZNR-B-H after adsorption of different H2 molecules.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research explores the structural, electronic, optical, and hydrogen storage properties of borophene nanoribbons (BNRs) with armchair (ANR-B-H) and zigzag (ZNR B-H) edges. Computational simulations optimized these structures, revealing that 7ZNR-B-H has a superior binding energy. Chemical modifications, such as fluorine passivation and functionalization, influenced bond parameters and quantum properties. Bilayer BNRs showed increased stability and enhanced electrical conductivity. Our study demonstrated promising hydrogen storage capabilities, with passivated and functionalized BNRs achieving suitable adsorption energies and a significant gravimetric storage capacity of 20.32 wt%, exceeding DOE standards. NH2 functionalization notably improved adsorption energy, enhancing potential for efficient hydrogen storage. Changes in absorption spectra post-H2 adsorption further highlight BNRs’ potential for hydrogen storage applications. These findings provide valuable insights into BNRs, paving the way for their use in electronic devices and hydrogen storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The global energy demand, largely met by non-renewable resources like petroleum, coal, and natural gas, has led to their rapid depletion and environmental damage [1,2]. This has driven the shift towards renewable energy, with hydrogen emerging as a promising alternative due to its abundance and clean combustion [3,4]. However, efficient hydrogen storage remains a significant challenge, especially for hybrid vehicles. The US-DOE (UnitedstatesDepartmentofEnergy requires future hydrogen storage systems to store 75 g of H2 per kg or liter [5,6]. Compressed gas and cryogenic liquid storage methods face issues like high weight, size, evaporative losses, and energy costs [6]. Solid-state storage technologies, such as metal hydrides and physisorption on high surface area materials, show promise but require further development [7]. Identifying optimal materials for hydrogen storage tanks is still challenging, despite extensive research [8,9]. Advances may come from doping, altering reaction pathways, or reducing particle size via techniques like ball milling with dielectric barrier discharge plasma [10–12].

Two-dimensional (2D) materials, characterized by their unique physical and chemical properties, have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their potential applications in various fields, including energy storage, electronics, and catalysis[13]. Graphene, the first experimentally isolated 2D material, has demonstrated exceptional properties but faces limitations for hydrogen storage due to its inert surface[14]. This has prompted researchers to explore other 2D materials, such as boron-based materials, for hydrogen storage applications. 2D nanomaterials, exemplified by graphene, have revolutionized various technological applications, including sensors, batteries, capacitors, photocatalysts, and solar devices [15]. Graphene, known for its high surface area, exceptional electron mobility, and thermal conductivity, stands out as a wonder material with significant potential in transparent conductors, sensors, and energy devices [16–18]. The exploration of 2D materials has expanded beyond graphene[19,20], encompassing hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) [21], black phosphorus (BP) [22], and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) [23,24], each offering unique physical, optical, and electronic properties [25,26]. While graphene's planar structure is unmatched, other 2D materials like silicene [27], phosphorene [28], arsenene [29], and germanene [30] present distinct characteristics, impacting electronic devices, storage, and utilization. Doping graphene with boron and nitrogen enhances its conductivity and surface chemical activity, making it suitable for various applications [31,32]. Boron, known for its utility in hydrogen storage systems, faces challenges in forming complex hydrides [33–35]. Recent focus on theoretical predictions and experimental synthesis of all-boron nanomaterials, including boron nanotubes and nanoribbons, combined with metal adatoms, provides an alternative for hydrogen storage with reversible adsorption [36–38].

Borophene, a 2D boron-based nanomaterial, exhibits metallic behavior, unlike bulk boron, making it promising for rechargeable ion batteries, hydrogen storage, and sensors [39–41]. However, its dynamic instability requires stabilization through methods like substrate deposition on metals such as Au, Cu, Ag, or metal borides [42,43]. Borophene, a single-atom-thick sheet of boron atoms, has emerged as a promising candidate for hydrogen storage due to its high theoretical hydrogen storage capacity and unique electronic properties[44]. The synthesis of 2D boron sheets on various substrates has been demonstrated, showing potential for practical applications [45,46]. To assess the suitability of experimentally developed borophenes for hydrogen storage, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted [47–49]. DFT, due to its balance between accuracy and computational efficiency, has become a significant tool for determining hydrogen storage materials, providing crucial information for practical material selection in hydrogen technologies.

In this study, we investigate the structural stability, electronic properties, optical properties, and hydrogen storage capabilities of borophene nanoribbons (BNRs), a promising class of two-dimensional materials. Specifically, we explore the armchair (ANR) and zigzag (ZNR) edge configurations of BNRs, analyzing their behavior under various edge passivation and functionalization schemes. Our theoretical modeling reveals intriguing insights into the tunability of BNR properties, shedding light on their potential applications in nanoelectronics and energy storage. Of particular interest are the exceptional hydrogen adsorption characteristics observed in BNRs, suggesting their viability for efficient hydrogen storage solutions. This study not only advances our fundamental understanding of BNRs but also sets the stage for future experimental validations and technological innovations.

In this study, we explore the optimized structures, electronic characteristics, optical behaviors, and hydrogen storage potential of materials based on 7ZNR-B through density functional theory (DFT) simulations conducted with Gaussian 16 [50]. To ensure a comprehensive representation of the system, the hybrid B3LYP functional [51,52] is employed, which has been previously scrutinized and demonstrated to provide reliable insights into the optimized, electronic, and optical properties [53–56]. The selected 6-31G basis set [57] is utilized to strike a balance between computational accuracy and efficiency [58–60]. For an in-depth analysis of optical properties, time-dependent DFT calculations for the first fifty excited states are performed. Additionally, the electronic properties of 7ZNR-B-H following the adsorption of H2 molecules are investigated using the 6-31G/B3LYP level of theory. To further elucidate the electronic structure, the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) are computed employing the Multiwfn software [61]. This comprehensive approach allows us to gain valuable insights into the structural, electronic, and optical characteristics of 7ZNR-B-based materials, as well as assess their potential for hydrogen storage. Using the [62–65] TD-DFT/B3LYP/6-31G level of theory, the optical characteristics are investigated up to excitation to 50 excited states.

In Fig. 1 , we illustrate finite borophene nanoribbons (BNRs) both pre- and post-chemical modification, along with a depiction of the bilayer structure of BNR. Specifically, Fig. 1a and 1b clearly depict BNRs with armchair edges, where 3ANR-B-H and 4ANR-B-H correspond to nanoribbons with three and four hexagons on the armchair edge, respectively. Fig. 1c and 1d illustrate zigzag nanoribbons (ZNR-B-H), with a focus on accommodating variations in zigzag edge sizes. This is achieved by examining nanoribbons with three (3ZNR-B-H) and seven (7ZNR-B-H) hexagons along the zigzag edge providing a nuanced understanding of structural variations. The binding energies (BE) of 7ZNR-B-H, 3ANR-B-H, 4ANR-B-H, and 3ZNR-B-H are measured at 4.561, 4.454, 4.499, and 4.374 eV, respectively. Notably, owing to its superior binding energy, 7ZNR-B-H is selected for further chemical modifications, including edge passivation and functionalization, as illustrated in Fig. 1e and 1f. Moreover, 7ZNR-B-H is chosen to serve as a bilayer structure (Bi-ZNR-B-H) due to its elevated binding energy, as presented in Fig. 1g. These findings emphasize the significance of 7ZNR-B-H in the context of potential applications and pave the way for exploring its enhanced properties through tailored chemical modifications. All structures under study exhibited planar side views in Fig. 1, except for 3ZNR-BH, which showed minor deformations. we systematically explored the optimized structures of borophene nanoribbons (BNRs), focusing on armchair (ANR-B-H) and zigzag (ZNR-B-H) edge variations. As illustrated in Table 1 , an increase in armchair edge size, for instance, from 3ANR-B-H to 4ANR-B-H, resulted in subtle changes in bond lengths and angles. Notably, the binding energies of these structures were measured, with 7ZNR-B-H exhibiting the highest value at 4.561 eV. Chemical modifications, exemplified by the passivation with fluorine in 7ZNR-B-F and functionalization in 7ZNR-B-H-CO, induced distinct alterations in bond lengths and angles. For instance, the B1-B2 bond length increased from 1.736 Å in 7ZNR-B-H to 1.971 Å in 7ZNR-B-F. Introducing diverse functional groups such as CN, CO, NH2, NO, OH, SH, and CHO, and exploring bilayer structures (Bi-7ZNR-B-H, Bi-7ZNR-B-F) further influenced the quantum parameters. While most structures, including 7ZNR-B-H and 7ZNR-B-H-CO, maintained planarity as evidenced by the close-to-180-degree dihedral angles, deviations were observed in specific cases such as 3ZNR-B-H, where the dihedral angle was 22.656 degrees. The comprehensive analysis provided in Table 1, anchored by examples and values, offers valuable insights into the structural intricacies of BNRs, crucial for understanding their electronic and chemical properties in potential applications.

In our investigation, we conducted a thorough evaluation of the stability of single-layer Boron Nitride Ribbons (BNRs) and their associated materials. Our approach involved calculating binding energies (BEs) using the formula: BE = (NBEB + NHEH + NNEN + NCEC + NOEO + NFEF + NSES − Et)/Nt. Here, NB, NH, NN, NC, NO, NF, NS, and Nt represent the numbers of B, H, N, C, O, F, and S and the total number of atoms, respectively. EB, EH, EN, EC, EO, EF, ES, and Et correspond to the total energies of B, H, N, C, O, F, and S atoms and the final compound, respectively. Our analysis of the calculated BEs revealed the robust stability of single-layer BNRs and their associated materials. The positive BE values, ranging from 4.374 to 5.232 eV, signify the favorable formation of these structures. These values have been tabulated for each structure in Table 1. Further scrutiny of the calculated BEs in Table 2 indicates that 7ZNR-B-OH exhibits greater stability compared to 3ZNR-B-H. Comparative analysis of the BE of 7ZNR-B-H with other single layers in Table 2 reveals that passivated groups such as F, OH, and SH, as well as functional groups (CHO, CN, CO, NH2, NO, OH, and SH), enhance the stability of 7ZNR-B. Moreover, we selected 7ZNR-B-F and 7ZNR-B-H as representatives to investigate the impact of a bilayer structure on the BE of the single layers. In this examination, the BE per layer was calculated using (BE = ((E1st-layer + E2nd-layer) − Et)/n). where E1st-layer is the electronic energy of the first layer, E2nd-layer is the electronic energy of the second layer, Et is the total energy of the bilayer and n is the number of the layer. The BE values for Bi-7ZNR-B-H and Bi-7ZNR-B-F were found to be 5.801 and 8.628 eV, respectively. This indicates that the bilayer structure enhances the BE of both 7ZNR-B-H and 7ZNR-B-F, establishing its higher stability compared to the single layer. In summary, our findings underscore the stability of single-layer BNRs and their related materials, highlighting the influence of passivated and functional groups. Additionally, the investigation into bilayer structures reveals an increased stability, providing valuable insights for future applications in materials science. Moreover, ab initio molecular dynamics simulations are used to assess the thermal stability of the Borophene nanoribbons. The simulations were performed using the atom-centered density matrix propagation (ADMP) molecular dynamics model simulations [66,67] for 10 fs at 500 K. Fig. S1 displays the time evolution of the potential energy and bond length at 500 K for selected structures. The trajectories show a small variation in the potential energy and the bond lengths at 500 K indicating the thermal stability at this high temperature.

Important quantum stability chemical (QSC) parameters like dipole moment (μ), chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), and chemical hardness (η) were calculated using the HOMO energy (EH) and the LUMO energy (EL). These QSC parameters are obtained from the following equations

[68] and collected in Table2. In our investigation, we have observed a notable correlation between molecular structures and electronic properties, particularly focusing on the parameter μ, which signifies the inherent asymmetry in electronic charge distribution. Among the structures examined, Bi-7ZNR-B-F displayed the highest μ value, indicating a pronounced intramolecular charge transfer. This trend was followed closely by 7ZNR-B-H-CN. Conversely, Bi-7ZNR-B-H exhibited the most negative ρ value, suggesting a heightened propensity for electron escape, consistent with its elevated electronegativity. Furthermore, Bi-7ZNR-B-H displayed the highest χ value, emphasizing its strong electron-attracting properties. In terms of chemical hardness (η), 4ANR-B-H exhibited the highest value, indicating greater resistance to charge transfer, while 7ZNR-B-F showed the lowest hardness, suggesting reduced resistance to charge transfer.

To explore the impact of structural modifications on electrical conductivity, we employed the equation
σ
α
e x p
(
- E
g
2 K T
)
[69–74], where σ represents electrical conductivity, k is Boltzmann's constant, T is the thermodynamic temperature, and Eg corresponds to the band gap value. Smaller Eg values at a given temperature result in higher conductivity. The computed electrical conductivity values are summarized in Table 2. Noteworthy findings include the substantial enhancement of electrical conductivity in BNR through increased zigzag edges and passivation of 7ZNR-B with F. Specifically, 7ZNR-B-F exhibited the highest electrical conductivity among all compounds studied, while 4ANR-B-H displayed the lowest. Additionally, functionalization of 7ZNR-B-H resulted in decreased electrical conductivity, observed in 7ZNR-B-H-CN and 7ZNR-B-H-CO. In the examination of bilayer structures, our findings highlight a noteworthy trend in the electrical conductivity of Bi-7ZNR-B-H and Bi-7ZNR-B-F compared to their single-layer counterparts. Interestingly, both bilayer configurations exhibit lower electrical conductivity when compared to the corresponding single-layer structures. This observation suggests that the introduction of a second layer, specifically in the form of Bi-7ZNR-B-H and Bi-7ZNR-B-F, results in a reduction in electrical conductivity. This outcome is significant in understanding the influence of bilayer arrangements on the electronic properties of the nanotube-based materials studied. The decrease in electrical conductivity in the bilayer structures may arise from interlayer interactions, altered charge transfer dynamics, or other factors associated with the stacking of nanoribbon layers. Further analysis and exploration of the specific mechanisms underlying this phenomenon are warranted to comprehensively elucidate the impact of bilayer configurations on the electrical properties of these nanotube systems. In summary, our study reveals a nuanced relationship between molecular structures, electronic properties, and electrical conductivity. The results underscore the potential for tailored structural modifications to significantly influence and optimize the electronic characteristics of BNR-based materials, offering valuable insights for applications in electronic devices. The electrical conductivity of BNRs, particularly after functionalization and passivation, plays a significant role in enhancing hydrogen adsorption. Higher electrical conductivity improves the interaction between the BNR surface and hydrogen molecules, facilitating easier adsorption and desorption processes [75]. This is particularly evident in the case of NH2 functionalization, which not only enhances conductivity but also increases the adsorption energy, leading to more efficient hydrogen storage.

To investigate the electronic characteristics, we utilized spin-up and spin-down Total Density of States (TDOS) and examined the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) for our borophene nanoribbons. The TDOS, depicted in Fig. 2 (a–d), was derived through an in-depth analysis of the Gaussian output file using r Multiwfn software, which calculates molecular orbital contributions [76]. Our observations reveal that both occupied and unoccupied molecular orbitals primarily originate from B-atoms, evident in both armchair and zigzag borophene nanoribbons. The HOMO/LUMO distributions, presented in Fig. 2(e–l), further underscore this trend. Notably, the HOMO is predominantly localized on B-atoms, while the LUMO exhibits a similar distribution. For instance, in Fig. 2(f, h), the HOMO is concentrated on B-atoms at the zigzag edge, while the LUMO is situated on B-atoms on the opposite armchair side. This implies that B-atoms at the zigzag edge function as donors, whereas those at the armchair edge act as acceptors. The spatial distribution of HOMO/LUMO, illustrated in Fig. 2(e–l), emphasizes the localization of these orbitals on B-B bonds, indicative of a robust B-B bond formation. In Fig. 2(a–d), we present the calculated energy gaps for spin-up and spin-down states in 3ANR-B-H, 3ZNR-B-H, 4ANR-B-H, and 7ZNR-B-H based on the spin-up/down total density of states (TDOS) spectra. Remarkably, we observe uniformity in the spin-up/down energy gaps for 3ZNR-B-H, 4ANR-B-H, and 7ZNR-B-H. However, in the case of 3ANR-B-H, there exists a notable discrepancy between the energy gaps of the spin-up (0.872 eV) and spin-down (0.937 eV) states. The observed difference in energy gaps between spin-up and spin-down states in 3ANR-B-H, as opposed to the other examined systems, suggests the presence of spin-dependent effects influencing its electronic structure. Further investigation into the underlying mechanisms driving these differences could provide valuable insights into the unique properties of 3ANR-B-H and potentially inform its practical applications in spintronics or other related fields.

Chemical modifications play a crucial role in manipulating the electronic properties of 7ZNR-B-H, whether occurring naturally during experimental synthesis or through intentional interventions. This study investigates two primary modification strategies: (a) functionalization, which involves substituting a hydrogen atom with CHO, CN, CO, NH2, NO, OH, or SH groups at the B atom on the edge of 7ZNR-B-H, and (b) complete passivation of B atoms with F, OH, or SH atoms. The impact of these modifications on electronic properties is evident in the presented PDOS and HOMO/LUMO distributions (Fig. 3 (a–j) and Supplementary file Fig. S2(a–j)). The energy gap (Eg) is calculated by this equation: Eg = ELUMO-EHOMO where ELUMO and EHOMO are the energy of LUMO and HOMO respectively. Notably, passivation with F atoms results in a marginal decrease in the energy gap of 7ZNR-B-H from 0.564 eV to 0.524 eV, as illustrated in Fig. 3(a). Conversely, SH passivation and functionalization with CHO groups at B atoms along the edge of 7ZNR-B lead to an increase in the energy gap. Specifically, SH passivation raises the energy gap to 0.747 eV, while functionalization with CHO groups results in a gap of 0.621 eV (Fig. 2(b, and d)). These findings highlight the effectiveness of passivation and functionalization in tailoring the electronic properties of 7NR-B. Examining Fig. 3(a–j) and Fig. S2(a–j) reveals that the highest energy gap is observed in 7ZNR-B-SH (0.747 eV) due to passivated SH groups, while the lowest is observed in 7ZNR-B-H-CN (0.512 eV) compared to the energy gap of 7ZNR-B-H (0.564 eV). The arrangement of functionalized and passivated 7ZNR-B according to the values of Eg is as follows: 7ZNR-B-H-SH > 7ZNR-B-H-CO > 7ZNR-B-H-NO > 7ZNR-B-H-CHO > 7ZNR-B-H-OH > 7ZNR-B-H-SH > 7ZNR-B-OH > 7ZNR-B-H-NH2 > 7ZNR-B-H > 7ZNR-B-OH ˃7ZNR-B-H-CN. Hence, all passivated and functionalized 7ZNR-B show higher Eg values compared to 7ZNR-B-H, except 7ZNR-B-H-CN and 7ZNR-B-F. As shown in Fig. 3(f–j) and S1(f–j), the HOMO and LUMO are localized on the B-B bond, confirming the strong B-B bond. Additionally, passivated and functionalized groups like SH, OH, CO, and NO exhibit minimal participation. In conclusion, our study demonstrates the substantial influence of chemical modifications on the electronic properties of 7ZNR-B-H. The observed variations in the energy gap underscore the potential for precise tuning through functionalization and passivation strategies.

In this study, we investigate the hydrogen adsorption capabilities of 7ZNR-B-H and its passivated/functionalized counterparts (See Fig. 4 a–k), aiming to identify the most promising compound for hydrogen storage applications. Our calculations focus on evaluating the adsorption strength at the edge site of 7ZNR-B-H, as well as the impact of passivating groups (F, OH, and SH) and functionalizing groups (CHO, CN, CO, NH2, NO, OH, and SH) on the adsorption energy. The adsorption energy (Ea) is calculated using the formula[77]:
(1)
E a

Where

is the ground state energy of the NR after adsorbing nH2, and n is the number of H2 molecules.
E
NR
and
E
H 2
are the ground state energies of the NR and H2 separately. In Eq. (1), we calculate the adsorption energy (Ea) by subtracting the electronic energy of the reactants (ENR + nEH2) from that of the products (ENR + nH2). This result is then divided by the number of H2 molecules (n) to obtain the Ea value per H2 molecule. A negative Ea value indicates spontaneous adsorption, while a positive value indicates non-spontaneous adsorption. Our results, as presented in Table 3 , highlight the exceptional adsorption energy of H2 on 7ZNR-B-H at the edge, with a value of −0.746 eV. This falls within the optimal range (−0.2 to −0.7 eV) recommended by the US Department of Energy (DOE-US) for efficient hydrogen storage materials [77]. Interestingly, the introduction of passivated and functional groups leads to a significant decrease in Ea, except for F passivation and NH2 functionalization. The latter two exhibit an increase in adsorption energy, surpassing the DOE-US ideal range at −1.374 eV and −2.160 eV, respectively (Table 3). Notably, the highest adsorption is observed in 7ZNR-B-H passivated with F (7ZNR-B-F) and functionalized with NH2 (7ZNR-B-H-NH2), while the lowest is found in 7ZNR-B-H passivated with OH and SH, as well as functionalized with CHO, CN, CO, NO, OH, and SH groups. Despite the general trend of decreasing adsorption energies with passivation and functionalization, all groups remain within the DOE-US specified range. It is noteworthy that passivated F and functionalized NH2 exhibit an increase in H2 adsorption energy, albeit exceeding the DOE-US range. Therefore, 7ZNR-B-H, along with its SH and OH passivated forms, and CHO, CN, CO, NO, OH, and SH functionalized derivatives, are identified as promising candidates for hydrogen storage. Table 3 also provides insight into the adsorption distances, revealing a significant reduction after functionalization and passivation. For instance, the adsorption distance decreases to 2.42 Å in the case of 7ZNR-B-H-CHO. The observed trend in adsorption distance aligns well with adsorption energy, where lower distances correspond to higher adsorption energy and vice versa. To further explore the potential of 7ZNR-B-H, which falls within the DOE-US specified range, we plan to investigate the increase in the number of adsorbed H2 molecules as shown in Fig. 4(l–s), and assess their adsorption energies. This comprehensive study aims to contribute valuable insights to the development of efficient hydrogen storage materials. Upon investigating the impact of increasing the concentration of H2 molecules, expressed in terms of the number of H2 molecules ranging from 3 to 50, on the Ea values as depicted in Table 3, a notable trend emerges. As the number of H2 molecules increases, the Ea values decrease as well, reaching 0.576 eV for 7ZNR-B-H-50 H2. The repulsive forces between H2 molecules increase as the quantity of H2 molecules increases, which causes the drop in Ea. Based on the reviewer's comments, we calculated the Basis Set Superposition Error (BSSE) for the 7ZNR-B-H-H2 system as an example, which was found to be 0.0136 eV. The uncorrected adsorption energy (Ea) for this compound was initially calculated as −0.746 eV. To obtain a more accurate value, we subtracted the BSSE from the uncorrected adsorption energy, resulting in a BSSE-corrected adsorption energy of −0.732 eV. This correction is crucial for accurately representing the interaction energy, ensuring that our predictions regarding hydrogen storage capabilities are reliable.

The desorption temperature (TD) is a critical factor in hydrogen (H2) storage applications, as it denotes the temperature at which stored H2 molecules are released for practical utilization. The Van’t Hoff formula [78] (Eq. (2)) is employed to calculate TD:
(2)
T D
=
E a
K B
Δ S
R
- l n p
- 1
Here, Ea represents the adsorption energy per H2 molecule, KB is the Boltzmann constant, ΔS signifies the change in H2 entropy from gas to liquid phase, R is the universal gas constant, and p denotes the atmospheric pressure. The TD values for 7ZNR-B-H and its passivated/functionalized variants are provided in Table 3. The notably high TD values observed for 7NR-B-H and its derivatives suggest robust retention of adsorbed hydrogen at ambient temperatures, positioning them as superior candidates for hydrogen storage materials. Passivation with OH and SH groups decreases TD, whereas F passivation elevates TD values. Conversely, functional groups such as CHO, CN, CO, NO, OH, and SH lead to decreased desorption temperatures (776.71 K, 933.59 K, 836.65 K, 740.27 K, 752.99 K, and 712.59 K, respectively), while NH2 functionalization increases TD to 2760.91 K for the initial adsorption of H2. Furthermore, the TD value decreases with an increasing number of adsorbed H2 molecules on the 7ZNR-B-H substrate until it peaks at 735.47 K for 7ZNR-B-H-50 H2. The repulsive forces between H2 molecules rise as the quantity of H2 molecules grows, which causes the TD values to decrease.

The gravimetric storage capacity, a crucial parameter for assessing material applicability in hydrogen storage, is determined using Eq. (3) [79]:
(3)
wt % =
nw

× 100
Where n is the number of H2 molecules adsorbed,
w
H 2
is the molecular weight of a single H2, and
w
7 Z N R - B - H
is the weight of the finite 7ZNR-B-H. The gravimetric percentage is computed for the maximum adsorbed H2 molecules, which is 50 H2. We observed a gravimetric storage capacity of 20.32 wt%, significantly surpassing the US Department of Energy's (DOE) standard of 6.0 wt% [57], thereby positioning 7ZNR-B-H as a promising candidate for hydrogen storage devices.

Table 4 compares the gravimetric storage capacities of 7ZNR-B-H with various other materials, highlighting its superior performance with a capacity of 20.32 wt%, well above the DOE target. This positions 7ZNR-B-H as a leading candidate for hydrogen storage. Other materials like phosphorene (4.45 wt%) and MgH2 (7.60 wt%) show lower capacities, though the introduction of vacancies in MgH2 and LiH increases their storage potential to 9.81 wt% and 12.679 wt%, respectively. BC6Be also demonstrates a strong capacity of 11.63 wt%. Both Mg(BH4)2 and NaBH4 are notable, with Mg(BH4)2 achieving 14.80 wt%, while C2N-h2D and LiBH4 also perform well, at 11.62 wt% and 18.50 wt% respectively. Metal decoration of t-B4C4 with Ti and Li further enhances its capacity, reaching up to 13.32 wt%. Overall, 7ZNR-B-H's exceptional storage capacity underscores its potential as a top material for hydrogen storage applications, while other materials offer competitive but lower capacities, each with unique properties and challenges.

In Fig. 1a, we examine the absorption spectra of various borophene nanoribbons (BNRs), including 3ANR-B-H, 4ANR-B-H, 3ZNR-B-H, 7ZNR-B-H, 7ZNR-B-F, and 7ZNR-B-OH, illustrating the impact of increasing armchair and zigzag edge lengths, as well as the influence of fluorine (F) and hydroxyl (OH) passivated atoms. Fig. 1b delves deeper into the functionalized 7ZNR-B-H absorption spectrum, elucidating alterations following the introduction of different functional groups. Subsequently, Fig. 1c sheds light on the absorption spectra of 7ZNR-B-H after the adsorption of varying quantities of H2 molecules. These figures underscore how modifications in the armchair and zigzag edge lengths lead to distinct λmax values, indicating shifts in absorption spectra. Detailed insights into excited states, maximum wavelengths, and transition energies are provided in Table 5 , demonstrating the sensitivity of BNRs to edge lengths. For instance, in 4ANR-B-H, a blue shift occurs as λmax decreases from 939.74 nm (3ANR-B-H) to 725.38 nm, while in 7ZNR-B-H, a similar blue shift is observed as λmax decreases from 1267.33 nm (3ZNR-B-H) to 1168.47 nm, highlighting the influence of edge lengths on electronic transitions and energy levels. Furthermore, passivated and functionalized 7ZNR-B-H exhibits similar blue shifts; for example, λmax decreases from 1168.47 nm (7ZNR-B-H) to 742.79 nm (7ZNR-B-F), and 996.34 nm (7ZNR-B-H-NO), emphasizing the impact of passivation and functionalization (as illustrated in Fig. 5 a and b). Oscillator strength (f) and transition coefficient (TC) changes in Table 5 further underscore the intricate interplay between passivation, functionalization, hydrogen molecules, and BNR optical properties. In Fig. 5c, subtle changes in the absorption spectra of 7NR-B-H are observed upon the adsorption of diverse quantities of H2 molecules. Increasing H2 concentration induces a discernible small blue shift, indicating a shift in the maximum absorption peak towards shorter wavelengths. For example, in 7ZNR-B-H-H2, calculated values in Table 5 reveal an excited state (ES) of 15, a maximum wavelength (λmax) of 1199.37 nm, a transition energy (TE) of 1.03 eV, and an electronic transition (ET) from H to L + 1. Moving to adsorb 40 H2 molecules induces a further blue shift towards shorter wavelengths compared to 7NR-B-H. With an ES of 15, λmax of 1171.52 nm, TE of 1.06 eV, and electronic transition from H to L + 3, these values elucidate the nuanced optical alterations resulting from both passivation/functionalization and H2 adsorption on 7NR-B-H. In conclusion, these findings underscore the significance of hydrogen storage as it relates to the optical properties of nanoribbons. The observed alterations in absorption spectra following H2 adsorption suggest the potential for BNRs to serve as promising candidates for hydrogen storage applications. The optical properties of BNRs, especially changes in absorption spectra post-H2 adsorption, provide insight into the material's interaction with hydrogen. These changes reflect the alterations in electronic structure upon hydrogen adsorption, which are closely tied to the material's storage capacity. The optical transitions observed indicate that the material can effectively absorb and release hydrogen, contributing to its suitability as a hydrogen storage medium.

In conclusion, our comprehensive computational study sheds light on the structural, electronic, optical, and hydrogen storage properties of borophene nanoribbons (BNRs) and their derivatives, emphasizing armchair (ANR-B-H) and zigzag (ZNR-B-H) edge variations. Through systematic exploration and optimization of various configurations, we uncovered intriguing insights into the impact of chemical modifications, including edge passivation and functionalization, on the properties of BNRs. Our findings highlight the exceptional stability and promising electronic properties of 7ZNR-B-H, positioning it as a compelling candidate for further investigation. Chemical modifications not only influenced the structural parameters but also demonstrated the potential to tailor electronic properties, offering avenues for customized applications in electronics and nanotechnology. Moreover, our investigation into bilayer structures revealed enhanced stability and intriguing trends in electronic properties compared to single-layer configurations. This suggests the feasibility of utilizing BNRs in stacked architectures to harness unique electronic characteristics for advanced device applications. Furthermore, our hydrogen storage simulations unveiled promising adsorption capabilities of BNRs, with passivated and functionalized derivatives exhibiting suitable adsorption energies within the desired range recommended by the US Department of Energy (DOE). Notably, NH2 functionalization emerged as a particularly promising avenue for achieving efficient hydrogen storage, warranting further experimental validation.
